part_rules: Part 18 (ISM equipment, center frequency 6.78 MHz +/- 15.0 kHz). ITU-designated ISM band. Part 18 ISM equipment on designated frequencies permitted unlimited radiated energy within the ISM band. Part 15 low-power devices also operate here.
power_limit: Part 18 ISM: No radiated emission limits within band (unlimited for ISM equipment on designated frequencies). Part 15 intentional radiators: field strength limits per 15.209/15.225. Non-ISM devices must comply with Part 15 emission limits.
use_cases: Industrial RF heating and welding, dielectric heaters, induction heating, RFID (some HF systems), NFC-adjacent research, wireless power transfer research, scientific instruments.
adjacent_licensed: HF amateur radio (40m band nearby at 7.0-7.3 MHz), HF broadcasting, fixed service, maritime mobile. The 40m amateur band is immediately adjacent above 7.0 MHz.
interference_issues: ISM equipment can produce significant broadband emissions affecting HF amateur and broadcasting receivers. Relatively low usage density in modern deployments. HF propagation means interference can travel long distances.
regulatory_status: Stable ITU-designated ISM band. Low commercial interest compared to higher ISM bands.
notes: One of the original ITU-designated ISM bands. Primarily used for industrial heating applications rather than communications. Very narrow (30 kHz bandwidth).

part_rules: Part 18 (ISM equipment, center frequency 13.56 MHz +/- 7.0 kHz). ITU-designated ISM band. Part 15.225 also governs low-power devices in 13.110-14.010 MHz range with specific field strength limits.
power_limit: Part 18 ISM: Unlimited radiated energy within ISM band for ISM equipment. Part 15.225: Field strength limits for intentional radiators — up to 15,848 uV/m at 30m within 13.553-13.567 MHz (the ISM band itself gets highest allowed field strength). Devices outside ISM band but within 13.110-14.010 must meet lower limits.
use_cases: NFC (Near Field Communication) at 13.56 MHz, HF RFID (ISO 14443, ISO 15693), contactless payment systems (Apple Pay, Google Pay NFC), access control cards, library book tags, smart cards, wireless charging (Qi standard uses 13.56 MHz in some modes), industrial plasma generators, medical diathermy.
adjacent_licensed: 13.36-13.41 MHz: Fixed/mobile. 14.0-14.35 MHz: Amateur radio (20m band). HF broadcasting and fixed services nearby.
interference_issues: NFC intentionally very short range (< 10cm), minimal interference. RFID readers can produce measurable emissions at distance. Industrial ISM equipment (plasma generators) can create significant interference to HF services. Part 15.225 limits designed to protect adjacent amateur 20m band.
pending_rulemaking: None specific to this band. NFC/RFID standards continue to evolve (NFC Forum, ISO updates) but no FCC rulemaking pending.
regulatory_status: Stable ITU-designated ISM band. Heavy commercial use via NFC/RFID. Well-established regulatory framework.
notes: 13.56 MHz is arguably the most commercially important low-frequency ISM band due to NFC/RFID. Billions of NFC-enabled smartphones and contactless payment terminals operate at this frequency. The narrow 14 kHz ISM bandwidth is sufficient for NFC data rates (106-424 kbps). Also used in scientific applications including nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) at certain field strengths.
source: FCC 47 CFR 18.301, 47 CFR 15.225; ITU Radio Regulations; NFC Forum specifications

part_rules: Part 18 (ISM equipment, center frequency 27.12 MHz +/- 163.0 kHz). ITU-designated ISM band. Overlaps with Citizens Band (CB) radio frequencies (Part 95 Subpart D, 26.965-27.405 MHz). Part 15 devices also present.
power_limit: Part 18 ISM: Unlimited radiated energy within ISM band for ISM equipment. CB radio (Part 95): 4W AM, 12W PEP SSB. Part 15 devices: standard field strength limits per 15.209.
use_cases: Industrial RF heating/welding (plastic welders, wood gluing), medical diathermy, CB radio (overlapping allocation), RC toys and models (27 MHz), some garage door openers (legacy), baby monitors (legacy), scientific instruments.
adjacent_licensed: 26.96-27.41 MHz: Citizens Band Radio Service (Part 95). 27.41-28.0 MHz: Fixed/mobile. 28.0-29.7 MHz: Amateur radio (10m band). HF broadcasting bands nearby.
interference_issues: ISM industrial equipment (especially plastic welders) notorious for producing broadband interference affecting CB radio, amateur 10m band, and HF broadcasting. CB radio and ISM share the same spectrum — mutual interference is inherent and longstanding. Plastic welding machines are one of the most common sources of harmful HF interference complaints to FCC.
pending_rulemaking: None specific to this band. CB radio rules have been largely static for decades.
regulatory_status: Stable ITU-designated ISM band. Shared with CB radio service. Industrial ISM use declining as newer heating technologies emerge.
notes: The 27.12 MHz ISM band has the widest tolerance of any ISM frequency (+/- 163 kHz, yielding 326 kHz bandwidth). Its overlap with CB radio is one of the oldest spectrum sharing conflicts in US radio regulation. The 27 MHz RC toy frequency is familiar to generations of hobbyists. Industrial use persists in manufacturing (plastic welding) but is declining. The band is historically significant as one of the original ISM allocations.
source: FCC 47 CFR 18.301, 47 CFR 95 Subpart D; ITU Radio Regulations

part_rules: Part 15.247 (spread spectrum/digital modulation), Part 18 (ISM equipment), Part 97.303 (amateur secondary). FHSS: >=50 hop channels for 1W, >=25 channels for 0.25W. Digital modulation: 1W max conducted output. Dwell time <=0.4s per channel within 20s (50-hop) or 10s (25-hop) period. 20dB bandwidth <250kHz requires 50 hops; >=250kHz requires 25 hops.
power_limit: FHSS: 1W conducted (>=50 hops) or 0.25W (25-49 hops). Digital modulation: 1W conducted output. Point-to-multipoint antenna gain >6dBi requires proportional power reduction. Fixed point-to-point: up to 24dBi antenna gain with no power reduction, above 24dBi reduce 1dB per 3dB gain. Max EIRP effectively 4W for general use, 158W for fixed P2P with high-gain antennas.
use_cases: LoRa/LoRaWAN (US915 plan, 64+8 uplink channels, 8 downlink), Zigbee 900, Z-Wave, Meshtastic, smart meters (Itron OpenWay, Landis+Gyr Gridstream RF using FHSS), RFID (Gen2 UHF 902-928), industrial telemetry, building automation, wireless security sensors, cordless phones (legacy), amateur radio repeaters and simplex.
adjacent_licensed: 896-901 MHz: SMR (Specialized Mobile Radio) upper band, licensed commercial dispatch. 901-902 MHz: Narrowband PCS. 928-929 MHz: Paging (licensed). 929-930 MHz: Paging. 930-931 MHz: Narrowband PCS. The SMR band at 896-901/935-940 MHz is actively used by commercial two-way radio services. Part 90 land mobile operations also present.
interference_issues: Dense smart meter deployments (millions of Itron/L+G units) create persistent FHSS interference floor across entire band. LoRa narrowband signals vulnerable to smart meter hopping collisions. Amateur 33cm operations secondary to ISM, must accept interference. Part 15 devices must not cause harmful interference to licensed users and must accept interference. Cordless phones (legacy 900MHz) cause localized interference. RFID readers near band edges can affect adjacent licensed services. Urban spectrum congestion increasing as IoT deployments scale.
pending_rulemaking: NextNav Petition for Rulemaking (filed April 16, 2024, FCC DA-24-776): NextNav (formerly Progeny, M-LMS licensee) proposes reconfiguring band to create 5MHz uplink (902-907 MHz) paired with 10MHz downlink (918-928 MHz) for flexible-use 5G PNT service, compressing all other users into 907-918 MHz (11 MHz). NextNav seeks to swap its M-LMS holdings for single nationwide 15MHz flexible-use license. Comments received Sept 5, 2024. Strong opposition from New America OTI, ARRL, LoRa Alliance, Itron, utilities, IoT industry. Would effectively eliminate Part 15 operations in 15 of 26 MHz.
regulatory_status: Currently unlicensed ISM under Part 18, shared with Part 15.247 spread spectrum devices and Part 97 amateur (secondary). NextNav petition under FCC review as of March 2026 — no NPRM issued yet but petition is actively docketed. Band status could change dramatically if NextNav petition proceeds to NPRM.
notes: This is the most contested ISM band in the US. NextNav acquired its M-LMS licenses through Progeny for approximately $2.4M and now seeks to convert them to flexible-use licenses worth potentially billions. The band supports an estimated 100+ million deployed IoT devices. ITU Region 2 only (Americas) — not available as ISM in Europe/Asia. Amateur radio operators have used 902-928 for weak-signal, repeaters, and ATV since 1985 allocation. The ARRL has filed opposition to NextNav petition citing harm to amateur operations.
source: FCC 47 CFR 15.247, 47 CFR 18.301, 47 CFR 97.303; FCC DA-24-776A1; New America OTI comments; ARRL

part_rules: Part 15.247 (spread spectrum/digital modulation), Part 18 (ISM — microwave ovens at 2.45 GHz center). FHSS: minimum 75 non-overlapping hop channels, 20dB bandwidth <=1MHz per channel. DSSS and digital modulation permitted. Part 15.249 also applies to some low-power devices.
power_limit: FHSS: 1W conducted output power (75+ hop channels). Digital modulation/DSSS: 1W conducted. Point-to-multipoint: reduce power 1dB for every 1dB antenna gain above 6dBi. Fixed point-to-point: 1W conducted, antenna gain up to 6dBi without reduction; above 6dBi reduce 1dB for every 3dB excess gain. Bluetooth: Class 1 (100mW), Class 2 (2.5mW), Class 3 (1mW).
use_cases: WiFi 802.11b/g/n/ax (channels 1-14, 20/40MHz), Bluetooth (Classic and BLE, 79 channels x 1MHz), Zigbee 802.15.4, Thread, Matter, microwave ovens (2.45 GHz magnetron), baby monitors, wireless cameras, cordless phones, industrial/scientific/medical heating equipment, amateur radio (Part 97, secondary).
adjacent_licensed: 2300-2400 MHz: WCS (Wireless Communications Service) and amateur. 2483.5-2500 MHz: Licensed mobile satellite service (Globalstar downlink). 2500-2690 MHz: BRS/EBS (educational broadband). The Globalstar boundary at 2483.5 is a hard regulatory edge.
interference_issues: Most congested ISM band globally. Microwave ovens radiate broadband noise across entire band during operation (duty-cycled magnetron). WiFi channel overlap (only 3 non-overlapping 20MHz channels: 1, 6, 11). Bluetooth frequency hopping collides with WiFi. Dense urban environments see dozens of competing APs. Cross-technology interference between WiFi, BT, Zigbee is persistent. Building penetration good at 2.4 GHz means interference propagates through walls.
pending_rulemaking: No major pending rulemaking specifically targeting 2.4 GHz ISM. Band is considered mature and stable. WiFi 7 (802.11be) primarily targets 5/6 GHz expansion. Some discussion of eventually sunsetting older 2.4 GHz-only devices but no formal proceeding.
regulatory_status: Stable unlicensed ISM. Universally allocated worldwide (ITU Radio Regulations). No anticipated changes to Part 15.247 rules for this band.
notes: The 2.4 GHz band is the most widely used unlicensed band globally. Approximately 10 billion+ active devices. Microwave ovens are the original Part 18 ISM use case — the 2.45 GHz center frequency was chosen because water molecules absorb energy efficiently at this frequency. Despite congestion, the band remains critical for IoT due to range/penetration characteristics superior to 5/6 GHz.
source: FCC 47 CFR 15.247, 47 CFR 18.301; IEEE 802.11 standards; Bluetooth SIG specifications

part_rules: Part 15.401-15.407 (UNII devices), Part 15.247 (5725-5850 MHz ISM portion only). Four sub-bands: UNII-1 (5.15-5.25 GHz) indoor only historically, now limited outdoor with filing requirement for >1000 APs; UNII-2A (5.25-5.35 GHz) requires DFS and TPC; UNII-2C (5.47-5.725 GHz) requires DFS and TPC; UNII-3/ISM (5.725-5.85 GHz) no DFS required.
power_limit: UNII-1: 1W EIRP (indoor), 4W EIRP (P2P with directional antenna). UNII-2A: 250mW conducted, 1W EIRP with TPC (-6dB capability required). UNII-2C: 250mW conducted, 1W EIRP with DFS/TPC. UNII-3: 1W conducted, up to 4W EIRP. Part 15.247 (5725-5850): 1W conducted, higher EIRP with directional antennas for P2P.
use_cases: WiFi 5 (802.11ac), WiFi 6 (802.11ax), WiFi 7 (802.11be), UNII fixed wireless broadband (WISPs use UNII-3 extensively), automotive radar research, cordless phones, industrial wireless.
adjacent_licensed: 5.0-5.15 GHz: Federal government (military/FAA radar). 5.35-5.47 GHz: Government radar gap (UNII-2B was never opened). 5.85-5.925 GHz: DSRC/C-V2X (automotive). Adjacent to 5.925 GHz WiFi 6E lower edge.
interference_issues: DFS false detections from weather radar (TDWR at airports) and military radar cause channel availability issues in UNII-2A/2C. Some APs have poor DFS implementations causing unnecessary channel vacations. UNII-3 congestion in suburban/rural areas from WISP deployments. Outdoor UNII-1 rules loosened but filing requirement adds compliance burden.
pending_rulemaking: Ongoing review of DFS requirements and potential relaxation for indoor-only devices. AFC (Automated Frequency Coordination) system being developed for 6 GHz may eventually inform 5 GHz policy. No major NPRM pending specifically for 5 GHz UNII bands.
regulatory_status: Stable unlicensed with DFS requirements for UNII-2A and UNII-2C. UNII-1 and UNII-3 most permissive. ISM portion (5.725-5.85 GHz) under Part 15.247.
notes: The 5 GHz bands provide significantly more spectrum than 2.4 GHz (up to 500+ MHz vs 83.5 MHz) but with reduced range due to higher frequency. DFS requirement is the primary operational constraint — devices must detect radar within 1 second and vacate within 10 seconds, with 30-minute non-occupancy period. UNII-2B (5.35-5.47 GHz) gap remains unavailable in the US.
source: FCC 47 CFR 15.401-15.407, 47 CFR 15.247; NTIA spectrum allocation table

part_rules: Part 15.407 (UNII-5 through UNII-8). UNII-5 (5.925-6.425 GHz): LPI indoor and AFC outdoor. UNII-6 (6.425-6.525 GHz): LPI indoor only. UNII-7 (6.525-6.875 GHz): LPI indoor and AFC outdoor. UNII-8 (6.875-7.125 GHz): LPI indoor only. VLP (Very Low Power) devices expanded to full 5.925-7.125 GHz band effective May 5, 2025 (FCC-25-8). AFC required for standard power outdoor operation.
power_limit: LPI (Low Power Indoor): 30 dBm max EIRP, 5 dBm/MHz PSD. Standard Power (AFC-controlled): 36 dBm max EIRP, 23 dBm/MHz PSD. VLP (Very Low Power): 14 dBm max EIRP, -8 dBm/MHz PSD. Client devices: LPI clients 24 dBm EIRP, SP clients 30 dBm EIRP, VLP clients 14 dBm EIRP.
use_cases: WiFi 6E (802.11ax), WiFi 7 (802.11be) — up to 59 new 20MHz channels, 14 new 80MHz channels, 7 new 160MHz channels, 3 new 320MHz channels. AR/VR wireless streaming, enterprise high-density deployments, residential mesh networks, industrial IoT.
adjacent_licensed: 5.85-5.925 GHz: DSRC/C-V2X (intelligent transportation). 7.125-7.25 GHz: Under study for potential expansion. Incumbents within band: Fixed microwave links (>90,000 licensed), broadcast auxiliary service (ENG), cable TV relay (CARS), fixed satellite service uplinks.
interference_issues: AFC system required to protect ~93,000 incumbent fixed microwave links from standard power outdoor operations. LPI operations must remain indoor and use contention-based protocols. VLP operations limited to very low power to avoid interference. Some concern about LPI leakage from buildings affecting outdoor incumbents. AFC database accuracy and update frequency are ongoing concerns.
pending_rulemaking: FCC-25-8 (adopted Jan 2025, effective May 2025): Expanded VLP to full 1200 MHz of 6 GHz band. Ongoing AFC implementation refinements. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (July 2025) exempted 5.9-7.1 GHz from mandatory reallocation for licensed use, protecting its unlicensed status. Potential future expansion above 7.125 GHz under study.
regulatory_status: Unlicensed under Part 15, with AFC requirement for standard power outdoor. VLP expansion across full band effective May 2025. Congress protected band from reallocation in OBBBA. Band is stable for unlicensed use.
notes: The 6 GHz band represents the largest single expansion of unlicensed spectrum in FCC history — 1200 MHz of contiguous spectrum. The US was first major country to open 6 GHz for unlicensed (April 2020), followed by South Korea, Brazil, Saudi Arabia, and others. Europe opened only lower 500 MHz (5.925-6.425 GHz). The AFC system is a novel spectrum sharing approach that may influence future bands. WiFi 7 320MHz channels are only possible in 6 GHz.
source: FCC 47 CFR 15.407; FCC-25-8A1; FCC Report and Order ET Docket 18-295; One Big Beautiful Bill Act

part_rules: Part 15.255. Communication devices: indoor and outdoor permitted with different power levels. Field Disturbance Sensors (FDS/radar): updated rules effective July 2023 with tiered power/duty-cycle structure. No licensing required. Atmospheric oxygen absorption at 60 GHz provides natural interference isolation.
power_limit: Communication (WiGig/WLAN): 40 dBm peak EIRP (indoor), 82 dBm peak EIRP (outdoor P2P with high-gain antenna), average power density 18 uW/sq.cm at 3m. FDS/Radar: 10 dBm EIRP (no restrictions), 20 dBm EIRP (indoor or with 50% duty cycle in 57-59.4/57-64 GHz), 30 dBm EIRP (outdoor fixed in 57-59.4 GHz), 14 dBm EIRP (22.7% duty cycle in 57-64 GHz).
use_cases: WiGig (802.11ad/ay) short-range multi-gigabit wireless, wireless display (WirelessHD), fixed P2P backhaul, in-vehicle radar (occupant detection), gesture recognition, industrial motion sensing, VR/AR wireless, data kiosks, wireless docking.
adjacent_licensed: 52-56 GHz: Government and non-government fixed/mobile allocations. 71-76 GHz: Licensed millimeter wave (E-band), shared. 76-77 GHz: Automotive radar. The 60 GHz band is relatively isolated due to oxygen absorption.
interference_issues: Minimal — oxygen absorption at 60 GHz (~15 dB/km) naturally limits range to tens of meters indoors, providing inherent interference isolation. Rain attenuation also significant. Main concern is ensuring FDS/radar devices with relaxed power limits do not impact communication devices in shared spectrum. Coexistence between WiGig and radar sensors addressed in 2023 rule update.
pending_rulemaking: FCC updated FDS/radar rules in 2023 (ET Docket 21-264, R&O adopted May 2023). No major pending proceedings. Band considered well-regulated for current use cases.
regulatory_status: Stable unlicensed. 14 GHz of contiguous spectrum (largest unlicensed allocation). 2023 radar rule updates were last significant change.
notes: 14 GHz of unlicensed spectrum — more than all other unlicensed bands combined. The 60 GHz oxygen absorption line makes this band uniquely suited for short-range, high-density deployments. IEEE 802.11ay supports up to 176 Gbps with channel bonding. Facebook/Meta Project ARIES explored 60 GHz for wireless backhaul. Market adoption slower than expected due to range limitations and competition from WiFi 6E/7.
source: FCC 47 CFR 15.255; FCC ET Docket 21-264; IEEE 802.11ad/ay specifications
